channel.title: Combat Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Combat Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Using Combat Skills Outside Of Combat
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using combat skills outside of combat.

This video can be seen as a companion piece to yesterday’s video about using non-combat skills in combat. And I’m filming this the day after the previous video, but the previous video is probably one you watched two days ago. I’ll link it below And I also want to remind you guys, it’s still June for me, so I haven’t seen your comments on that video. I thought I’d make this video before I saw your comments, so we can kind of compare our ideas on, in this case, usage making, utility for combat skills outside of combat. So you may want to watch the other one first. In any case, I’ll also start off by saying this has been done before. I know Fallout did it, I know The Outer Worlds did it.

item.title: Using Non Combat Skills In Combat
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using non-combat skills in combat.

This comes from a question from IE50 — IE50 - IE50, who asked: “I don’t know if you’ve done a video on this, but I was wondering about your thoughts on how traditionally non-combat skills can assist in combat. You’ve mentioned how skill-based games can result in players making dud characters because they end up sucking in combat. What about having those skills have related perks or actions that are valuable in combat? Or the more common option is having certain combat actions scale off. Non-combat skills Examples: intelligent scale skills, scale medicine, charisma, skills for team buffs, etc.

item.title: Action Points, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about action points, part two.

I did a video a few weeks ago on action points, Generated a lot of really good discussion in the comment section, including this comment from Matamwit who asked: I love this topic. I would like to ask what everyone thinks about, and then there’s five topics. What One?

What advantages and disadvantages are there if we separate, split movement points and action points in two separate pools? Number two: what do you think about barring action points from the next turn? Three: what about allowing one character to sacrifice their own points for another party member to take action with? Four: what do you think about skills and spells that require more action points than you currently have available and the character has to spend multiple turns if they want to perform the action? This is a good opportunity to lock more powerful skills behind multiple turn blocks and force opponents to focus the character that is preparing a bigger scout — uh, bigger spell. And number five: higher action point pools which allow for perks and items that increase the pool by a small amount without impacting game balance.

item.title: Real-Time Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about realtime Fallout.

Gkid 100 asked: can you make a video about what Fallout would have been like if you did make it realtime action? What would have been needed to come out of the game? What about the balance of the perks would have changed? Do you think multiplayer could have worked in the Fallout Universe? I’ve talked about remaking Fallout and also what would change today in two separate videos and I’ll link them below. So I just want to make it clear off the top that I would not have made Fallout real time, not back in 97, not today, in 2025, and this is despite — and I’ve mentioned this — interplay marketing approached me in 1996, a year before we came out, and they wanted Fallout to be me made real time because of Diablo. This they pushed several times and the way I finally got it — the stop — was just pointing out how much money I would need and time, and then they finally.

item.title: Action Points
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about action points.

This is an interesting question. It comes from tact DB who asks: where do you draw the line and how granular the action point should be for one’s game — AP resolution, if you will. Some RPGs use a base 100 AP for every character with equipment and status as MiFi modifiers. Everything you do, from opening your backpack to raising your weapon costs AP. Some actions even activate an AP drain, like moving with your Shield up costs more than just having it default.

item.title: Why I Wanted Luck In Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I wanted luck in Fallout.

It’s for me early February, I’ve been snowed in for a few days and this is a fun Friday video and I figured why not? If you remember, I talked about when Fallout had to be converted from gerps to special. Chris Taylor had a home brew system he wanted to use, which we later added perks to when Brian Fargo said: hey, I want something for people to buy, besid skills, but the attributes were just strength, perception, endurance, Charisma, intelligence and Agility. There was no luck I I talk about this in the how we made Fallout special vid. I really, really wanted luck to be in there because I had had a ton of fun with luck based characters in gerps, especially GP supers, and in Champions and in a few other tabletop role playing games that had luck in them. Um, if you watch my interplay after hours video, I talk about a character I had called lucky boy who bought all the different luck, uh, advantages or powers that were in the tabletop I was using and was just insanely fun to play. It wasn’t that he was good at anything, it’s just he frequently got lucky and scored critical hits or Critical with the skill and then anyone who tried to attack him frequently messed up and hurt themselves and other people around them. It was fun, because of that. I wanted in Fallout, I wanted and something like that. First of all, I wanted it as an attribute, because I remember when I brought it up people were like, well, we’re just throw in perk, a luck perk and I’m like, no, as a perk that means some people won’t have it. So when you go, what’s the luck on them?

item.title: Damage Types
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about damage types.

I honestly thought that I had a video on damage types. I didn’t, but I sure have talked about them a lot. I recently did a video on localized damage systems — I think it was last week, um — where I talked about damage types and even mentioned like Elemental damage. I didn’t really talk about what those were, and this led Juma Fallout to ask Tim, following up on the lineup thought: how do you handle different damage types? Always comes to mind Fallout with specific armors, like the Tesla armor or the EMP damage that only did Splash damage to the PC.

item.title: Localized Damage Systems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about localized damage systems.

What do I mean by that comes from a question from viewer Kan and Abel. Okay, putting Kane in there gives you one eyebrow rais, but the fact that you spelled AEL, AI, b, l gives you both eyebrows raised anyway. Kan and Abel asked: can you do a video on the pros and cons of localized damage? Like body parts have their own health pull and if, for instance, your leg is too injured, you can’t run or have a hard time walking. First of all, I chose this because this is a really fun topic to talk about, like it just opens up so many things when you’re talking about something so basic in a role playing game — and I’m going to talk about, in the context of a role playing game, something so basic as getting damaged. And I want to tell you, localized damage is way more than just talking about aim shots and crippling and stuff like that. In fact, it’s more than even having localized Health pools, because before I even talk about that, let’s talk about hit points for a second. Hit points are great.

item.title: Violence As The Default In AAA RPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about violence as the as the default in AAA RPGs.

It’s a very specific title because I mean all of this. I’m talking about violence. I’m talking about games that have violence as the default thing. That’s what they expect you to do, and I’m only talking about AAA RPGs because this came from a very good, thoughtful and well-written question by saber vs7, who asked: hi, Tim, I’m curious as to if you think games, while practically speaking, the RPG genre, specifically at the mainstream budget level — I know there’s plenty of Indies who are trying to be the change you want to see in this regard — will be able to evolve past the Paradigm of violence being the default way in which the player interacts with the world. Just so you know. This went on for a long time.

item.title: Enemy AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about enemy Ai, and this is to answer a question from driver 3899, who asked: any chance you could talk a little bit about how you approach thinking about enemy AI?

Is there a sweet spot between too complex and too simple in terms of behavior? Can you accidentally make enemies too smart for players, or is the performance hit the limiting factor and how complex you can make their behaviors? What are the most important things to keep in mind when designing the enemy AI?

item.title: Damage Numbers, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about damaged numbers again, because I already did a video on damage numbers, but that video was mostly about how they should appear in the HUD.

And apparently what a lot of you wanted was: how do you actually pick damage numbers? And by that you meant the range of damage numbers, because once you have a range of damage numbers — like do you go from 1 to 10, one to 20, one to 100, one to a th000? Once you pick that range, that leads to a r, a subsequent range of damage, of numbers for hit points and bonuses and weapon damage and things like that. So I’ve talked about damage numbers as a HUD. I’ve also talked about the ranges you should use for skills and attributes. This talk is going to be very similar because a lot of the things I said in the skill and attribute talks, which I’ll link below, are still valid here. Um, so, before I go about talking about the method, I use to do numbers, number ranges for damage, let me talk about just some stuff about numbers. Big numbers make people happy, when they hit something and they see two, they’re not as happy as when they see 20 or 200 or 2,000 or 20,000. Now you may go wait, if the monster has 10 hit points, who cares if you’re doing two versus the monster has 10,000 hit points and you do 2,000?

item.title: Damage Numbers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about damage numbers — yes, those little numbers that fly off of people when you hit them in games — because that’s actually an interesting topic to talk about, and

I got asked a question about it. Zandatsu asks Dam. Damage numbers are a feature that I feel sometimes gets treated as being insignificant. If you have any stories about the specific implementation of a small thing like this and how all those little choices get made, I’d love to hear about it in a video. Are some things like this left entirely up to one person to implement, or is there always multiple people coming to a consensus over what works best? Are there things that feel unimportant during development but, upon reflection, should have been given much more thought in terms of their implementation? I feel like damag numbers may be one of those things. You are right, damaged numbers are something that frequently — especially a, I’d say, in my older games — got left to like, isn’t? It obvious we’re just have a number fly up when they get hit and or, if we do that at all?

item.title: Enemy Progression
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about enemy progression.

Stagi Ayatollah asks: well, says a good topic for the youngans might be beneficial — is your thoughts on how to handle enemy scaling, something that’s fumbled a lot these days, IMO, perhaps in a border topic of how to efficiently handle enemies in general. I replied to this one and said I didn’t really want to talk about enemy scaling, but I would talk about enemy progression because I’ve mentioned in another video that I don’t like level scaling. I don’t like it when the enemies are adjusted automatically to fit your level and I also don’t like that how it’s often done, just as here are a bunch of extra hit points and better armor and better weapons, to the point where you’re wondering why is a bandit who’s literally carrying around 40,000 gold worth of weapons and armor attacking me?

item.title: The Origin of VATS
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the origin of Vats in Fallout and I’ll start by saying we didn’t call it bats in Fallout 1 and two.

That’s a Fallout 3 term, but it’s just what everybody calls it. Now, and this is me answering a question from Vladimir in Germany who asks: I always wonder how this feature that was later known as Vats came to be. Where did it come from?

item.title: Bosses
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about bosses and not me, even though I’m a boss.

Yo, I don’t know why I did that. Um, Matthew bazin 6432 asks: I would like to hear more of your thoughts on bosses and other special NPCs. In other words, how have games handled and how could they handle these enemies in unique and interesting ways? I think I talked about this a little in a Q&A. I did um a few months ago, but you know what? Let’s just do a whole video on it, because they’re interesting. Bosses are interesting, they’re interesting encounters. To me. Um, for me as a designer, a boss is a place where the designer is talking right to the player and going: okay, you think you’ve learned how to play, huh, well, bring it, because we’re going to rumble generally. Now, it’s interesting. I didn’t plan to use used them in The Outer Worlds. In fact, I told Charlie staple is the lead designer. I I said I don’t really.

item.title: Combat Coding
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about combat, like technical how to code combat kind of thing, like I’m going to talk data structures and event handling.

I wasn’t going to do this, but I’ve done some technical talks in the past and people really liked it and I was trying to think of what I could talk about that was General enough that it would be applicable to a lot of you but, at the same time, really interesting. And I hit upon the combat stuff because a — whoa, okay, maybe I should start editing. I’m gonna leave that in there. Yes, um, I I want to talk about combat because a — every RPG has combat and B — I did this a bunch of different ways. I did it one way for bar tail construction set and another way for Fallout, even though they were both turn-based, and then another way for Arcanum, and I finally settled on this method, which I really like.

item.title: Arcanum Combat: Real Time vs Turn Based
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arcanums, various combat modes, the real time, turn-based and fast turn based, kind of what the ideas were behind that, why it’s even in the game, um, what I wish

I had done differently. So go back to 1998. We had originally specced out Arcanum before we talked to Sierra.

Um. We had a rough outline of what we wanted to do and we were planning to make it turn based. We wanted to do turn based. We coming off Fallout, we understood turn based, turn-based RPGs have something that I really like, which is, as I’ve said in another video, when I cast a spell, especially a spell that has an area of effect, and especially in an isometric game, when things are moving around while I’m casting it, that drives me insane. I mean I liked Baldur’s Gate, but that it drove me insane to try to cast like a fireball in that spell.

item.title: Arcanum Criticals
item.description:
Hi everyone, this is Tim. Today I want to talk about how criticals work in Arcanum.

Sounds like a simple question. I completely went down the rabbit hole in arcanum’s code. I think I mentioned once that Arcanum was very much our everything in the kitchen sink game, and I think every single idea that anyone ever had about criticals — whether it was combat or not, whether it was success or failure — went into Arcanum. So if this is something that interests you keep listening, because I have an awful lot of stuff to go over.

item.title: Fallout Criticals
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to explain exactly how criticals work in Fallout, and by criticals I mean both critical successes and critical failures.

This is sort of a follow-up, because someone asked about this when I explained how all the Fallout attributes work. So I want to go through exactly how we coded it. My notes are really clear on this, so I’ll go through um, just so you know.
